skill.name: product-manager-toolkit
skill.description: Comprehensive toolkit for product managers including RICE prioritization, customer interview analysis, PRD templates, discovery frameworks, and go-to-market strategies. Use for feature prioritization, user research synthesis, and product documentation.
skill.summary: Comprehensive product management toolkit with RICE prioritization, customer interview analysis, PRD templates, and go-to-market frameworks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 71 static findings evaluated as false positives. The skill contains legitimate Python CLI scripts for product management workflows. Documentation shows example commands in markdown code blocks (not actual execution). Market sizing references (TAM/SAM/SOM) were incorrectly flagged as Windows SAM. Generic terms triggered weak crypto flags. File operations are intentional CLI functionality for reading user-provided transcript files.

high_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Flagged
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged markdown code blocks showing 'python scripts/...' as shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples, not actual command execution. Located in SKILL.md lines showing example CLI usage.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are markdown code blocks containing documentation examples, not actual shell commands. The scanner misinterprets markdown backticks as shell backtick syntax.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Scanner incorrectly flagged common words like 'Security', 'Design', 'Principles', 'High-Level' as weak crypto algorithms. No cryptographic code exists in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic code found. Scanner incorrectly matches common business terms like 'Security', 'Design', 'Principles' as weak crypto references.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Windows SAM Database
high_findings.description: Line 27 of prd_templates.md contains 'TAM, SAM, SOM' which refers to market sizing (Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Available Market, Serviceable Obtainable Market), not Windows Security Account Manager.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a standard business/marketing acronym for market size analysis, completely unrelated to Windows system files.

high_findings.title: False Positive: HTTP Client Library / Network Access
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'requests.append()' as HTTP library usage. This is a Python list variable named 'requests' storing analysis results, not the HTTP requests library.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Variable named 'requests' stores extracted feature requests from interview analysis. No network requests made by this script.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Certificate/Key Files
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged 'keys = prioritized[0].keys()' as certificate keys. This is simply extracting dictionary keys for CSV output formatting.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Python dictionary .keys() method used for CSV column headers, not cryptographic keys or certificate files.

high_findings.title: Legitimate: File Input Operations
high_findings.description: Scripts accept file paths from command line arguments to read user-provided transcript files. This is expected CLI tool behavior.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: CLI tools legitimately read input files specified by users. Not a vulnerability - this is the intended function of transcript analysis and CSV prioritization tools.

content.value_statement: A comprehensive toolkit for product managers to prioritize features using RICE scoring, analyze customer interviews, and create professional PRD documents. Streamlines product discovery and planning workflows.
actual_capabilities: Prioritize features using RICE scoring methodology with capacity planning
Analyze customer interview transcripts to extract pain points, feature requests, and sentiment
Generate professional PRD documents from structured templates
Calculate reach, impact, confidence, and effort scores for features
Create sample CSV files for testing prioritization workflows
limitations: Requires Python 3.6+ to run CLI scripts locally
RICE scoring requires manual input for reach, impact, confidence, and effort values
Customer interview analysis works best with semi-structured interview transcripts
Does not integrate with external project management tools

use_cases.title: Feature Prioritization Workshop
use_cases.description: Run a feature prioritization session using RICE scoring. Generate a CSV of features with scores, then use the script to rank and plan roadmap capacity.

use_cases.title: Customer Interview Synthesis
use_cases.description: After customer interviews, paste transcript into a text file and run the analyzer to extract actionable insights, sentiment, and feature requests.

use_cases.title: PRD Documentation Creation
use_cases.description: Use PRD templates to document new feature requirements. Choose from standard, one-page, or feature brief formats based on scope.
use_cases.target_user: Technical product managers and PM leads

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize My Feature List
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RICE prioritization method to help me score these features. For each feature, I need to estimate: Reach (users per quarter), Impact (massive/high/medium/low/minimal), Confidence (high/medium/low), and Effort (person-months). Here's my feature list: [paste features]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a list of feature requests and need to prioritize them objectively

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Customer Interview
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a customer interview transcript. Please analyze it and extract: 1) Key pain points with severity, 2) Explicit feature requests, 3) Sentiment (positive/negative/neutral), 4) Jobs to be done, 5) Key quotes. Here's the transcript: [paste transcript]

prompt_templates.title: Draft PRD from Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Product Requirements Document for a new feature. I have these inputs: Problem statement [describe], Target users [define], Success metrics [list], Timeline [specify]. Please structure this as a standard PRD using the template format.
prompt_templates.scenario: Converting discovery findings into formal documentation

prompt_templates.title: Capacity Planning for Quarter
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 20 prioritized features with RICE scores and effort estimates. Our team has capacity for 15 person-months this quarter. Which features should we commit to? Show me the portfolio breakdown of quick wins vs big bets.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quarterly roadmap planning and capacity allocation

output_examples.input: Feature list: Login improvement (reach 5000, impact high, confidence 80%, effort 3), Search redesign (reach 10000, impact medium, confidence 90%, effort 5), Notification system (reach 2000, impact massive, confidence 60%, effort 8)
output_examples.output:
RICE Scores:
1. Search Redesign: Score 180 (Reach 10000 × Impact 0.5 × Confidence 0.9 ÷ Effort 5)
2. Login Improvement: Score 133 (Reach 5000 × Impact 0.5 × Confidence 0.8 ÷ Effort 3)
3. Notification System: Score 30 (Reach 2000 × Impact 1.0 × Confidence 0.6 ÷ Effort 8)

Recommendation: Focus on Search Redesign and Login Improvement for Q1.

output_examples.input: Customer said: 'The current dashboard is so slow, it takes forever to load. I really need real-time notifications when orders come in. The search function is useless - I can never find what I need. But I love the new reporting feature!'
output_examples.output:
Pain Points (Severity: High):
- Slow dashboard loading
- Poor search functionality

Feature Requests (Priority: High):
- Real-time notifications for orders

Sentiment: Mixed (positive for reporting, negative for performance)

Key Quote: 'The current dashboard is so slow, it takes forever to load'

best_practices: Gather cross-functional input when scoring RICE components to improve accuracy
Use customer interview analysis to validate pain points before prioritizing solutions
Start with one-page PRD for small features, use standard PRD for complex initiatives
Review and update RICE scores quarterly as confidence and reach data improves
anti_patterns: Do not skip confidence scoring - it prevents overconfidence in uncertain estimates
Do not use RICE for all decisions - combine with strategic fit and technical dependencies
Do not write PRDs without user research - validate problems first
Do not ignore effort estimates from engineering - use realistic team velocity

faq.question: What is RICE prioritization?
faq.answer: RICE is a scoring framework: Reach (users affected) × Impact (effect on user) × Confidence ( certainty of estimates) ÷ Effort (resources needed). Higher scores indicate higher priority.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies?
faq.answer: The scripts require Python 3.6 or higher. No external packages needed - they use only standard library modules.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The toolkit works as a CLI companion. You can use Claude to draft features and PRDs, then run the Python scripts for numerical scoring and analysis.

faq.question: How do I estimate Reach for a feature?
faq.answer: Reach = number of users who will benefit per quarter. For existing features, use analytics data. For new features, estimate based on user research and market size.

faq.question: What file formats does the interview analyzer accept?
faq.answer: The script reads plain text (.txt) files. Paste your interview transcript into a text file and pass it as a command line argument.

faq.question: Can I export results to JSON or CSV?
faq.answer: Yes. The rice_prioritizer.py supports --output json or csv flags. Default output is to console for easy copying.
